title: Opportunity-preemption operator gap candidate
notes: Candidate representation generated after the first adversarial pass. It tests whether opportunity-normalized absence, preemption, recoverability, and operator-value structure can jointly model gaps without letting frequency or OVMG win by definition.

edges.rationale: Observed frequency must be interpreted against the number of contexts in which the candidate form could have occurred.
Persistent absence across high-opportunity environments is evidence against licensing, while low opportunity leaves absence underdetermined.
Frequency is evidence for licensing only after opportunity mass is considered; it is not licensing itself.
In this candidate, some licensing failures involve missing or mis-set operator values in a restricted public-update repertoire.
Recoverable intended meanings can improve reported acceptability even when licensing remains doubtful.
Processing burden can lower reported acceptability and must be separated from licensing.
Analogical support can make a candidate form producible or tempting even before it is broadly licensed.
Community licensing predicts ordinary production availability in the relevant context.
Licensing state predicts whether speakers or editors are pressured to reformulate the expression.
Repair pressure is evidence about perceived licensing failure, while also being shaped by ideology and genre in fuller models.
Acceptability is retained as a judgment proxy rather than collapsed into the grammaticality target.
Licensing is a central source of evidence for attribution but still needs adversarial testing against non-operator explanations.
